results.name: Qh4 Blunder Trap
results.pgn: e4 e5 Nf3 Nc6 d4 exd4 Nxd4 Qh4 Nb5 Qxe4+ Be2 Kd8 O-O
flags: WEAK_TRAP: student only -1 material, no mate (kind=trap, expected ≥ +3 or mate)
results.explanation: Here's the punishment that turns one bad move into a lost game. After 4...Qh4+? 5.Nb5 Qxe4+ 6.Be2 — Black faces a fork-fear problem. The CORRECT move is 6...Bb4+ (forcing a tempo back). But many Black players panic about 6...c7 hanging to Nxc7+ and reflexively play 6...Kd8?? running the king. Now it's over: castling rights gone permanently, the h8-rook locked behind its own king for the next 30 moves, and the king on d8 sitting on a half-open file. Just 7.O-O and develop calmly — Nf3, Nc3, d4, Bf4 — your pieces flow out while Black's king is the worst-placed piece on the board. This is what a tempo loss in the opening actually looks like.

results.name: Poisoned Pawn Trap
results.pgn: e4 c5 Nf3 d6 d4 cxd4 Nxd4 Nf6 Nc3 a6 Bg5 e6 f4 Qb6 Qd2 Qxb2 Rb1 Qa3 e5 dxe5 fxe5 Nfd7 Ne4 h6 Bh4 Qxa2 Rd1 Qd5 Qe3 Qxe5
results.explanation: Fischer's masterpiece — Black grabs the b2-pawn with the queen, knowing it's theoretically poisoned, then dares White to prove it. The point: after Rb1 Qa3 (the only square — Qa3 is the magic prophylaxis preventing Nb5), Black has gained a pawn and the time spent chasing the queen costs White the initiative. After 18 moves of forced theory, Black ends up TWO pawns up with a defensible position. ONLY play this if you've memorised the line — 'I'll figure it out at the board' loses every time.

results.name: f5-f4 Pawn Storm Crush
results.pgn: d4 Nf6 c4 g6 Nc3 Bg7 e4 d6 Nf3 O-O Be2 e5 O-O Nc6 d5 Ne7 Ne1 Nd7 f3 f5 Be3 f4 Bf2 g5 Nd3 Ng6 c5 Nf6 cxd6
results.explanation: The dream sequence. After the standard 10...f5 11.Be3 (White inserts the bishop on the way to defence), Black plays the killer 11...f4! — sealing the kingside, gaining the g3-square forever. White's bishop must move (12.Bf2) and Black follows up with the dagger 12...g5! The kingside pawn wave is locked and unstoppable. Now Black brings up the rest: 13.Nd3 (White rerouting too slowly) 13...Ng6 (the knight heads for f4 via h4 or directly), 14.c5 — White finally tries the queenside break, but it's too late. 14...Nf6 (Black keeps developing) 15.cxd6 — but the c-file opening for White comes after Black already has all the attacking pieces lined up. From the resulting position, …Rf6 and …Qe8-h5 finish the picture. The KID at its absolute best: locked centre, unstoppable kingside avalanche, every Black piece pointed at one square.

results.name: Exchange Qa4+ Counter
results.pgn: d4 Nf6 c4 g6 Nc3 d5 cxd5 Nxd5 e4 Nxc3 bxc3 Bg7 Qa4+ Nd7 Nf3 O-O Be2 c5 O-O cxd4 cxd4 Nc5 dxc5 Bxa1
flags: UNCLASSIFIED: no entry in trap-line-classifications.json
results.explanation: A textbook Grünfeld trap. After 5.e4 Nxc3 6.bxc3 Bg7 7.Qa4+ — looks like a useful developing check, but Black's calm 7...Nd7 blocks AND defends c5 simultaneously. Black continues 8.Nf3 O-O 9.Be2 c5 striking at the centre on schedule. White's natural 10.O-O cxd4 11.cxd4 sets up the trap moment: Black plays 11...Nc5! attacking the queen on a4. White's 'obvious' 12.dxc5? grabs the knight back — and now Black plays the killer 12...Bxa1! grabbing the rook on the long diagonal. Why doesn't the queen recapture? Because the queen on a4 can't reach a1: the a2-pawn blocks the a-file, and the c1-bishop blocks the back rank. The bishop on a1 is permanently safe. Black has won the exchange. The lesson: in the Grünfeld, the Bg7 is always aiming at a1 — every time White's rook is undefended, calculate Bxa1.

results.name: Overextended Center Strike
results.pgn: d4 Nf6 c4 g6 Nc3 d5 cxd5 Nxd5 e4 Nxc3 bxc3 Bg7 Bc4 O-O Ne2 c5 O-O Nc6 Be3 Bg4 f3 Na5 Bd3 cxd4 cxd4 Be6 d5 Bxa1 Qxa1 f6
results.explanation: The Grünfeld's signature exchange sacrifice. White spends moves 13-27 building a centre with Bc4, Ne2, O-O, Be3, f3, Bd3, and finally 27.d5 — the dream push. But the Bg7 has been pointed at a1 since move 6, and the d4-square that was protecting a1's diagonal just emptied with 25.cxd4 and then 27.d5. Black plays 27...Bxa1! collecting the rook. White must recapture 28.Qxa1 (the d1-queen slides along the now-empty back rank: c1 was vacated by Be3 on move 19, b1 was empty all game). Material count: Black is up the exchange. Yes, Black gave up the fianchetto bishop and the kingside dark squares now lack a defender — but with a clean rook-for-bishop trade and White's d5-pawn isolated far from queening, the exchange wins. The Bg7-versus-a1 dynamic is what makes the entire Grünfeld worth playing.

results.name: Early Queen Trap on d1
results.pgn: d4 Nf6 c4 g6 Nc3 d5 cxd5 Nxd5 e4 Nxc3 bxc3 Bg7 Nf3 c5 Be3 Qa5 Qd2 O-O Rc1 cxd4 cxd4 Qxd2+ Nxd2 Rd8 d5 e6
results.explanation: The principled mainline with a punchline. After the queen trade on d2, White's d5 push tries to keep the centre intact — but …Rd8 hits the d-pawn and …e6 undermines it. White is now overextended: the d5-pawn requires defence that disrupts the rest of the position. A textbook example of how the Grünfeld endgame favours the side that ATTACKS the centre, not the side that owns it.

results.name: Poisoned b7 Pawn Queen Trap
results.pgn: d4 d5 c4 c6 Nf3 Nf6 Nc3 e6 Bg5 dxc4 e4 b5 e5 h6 Bh4 g5 Nxg5 hxg5 Bxg5 Nbd7 exf6 Bb7 g3 c5 d5 Qb6 Bg2 O-O-O
results.explanation: The Anti-Moscow's most beautiful resource. After White sacrifices a piece for the kingside attack, Black calmly develops the c8-bishop to b7 and CASTLES QUEENSIDE — the d5-pawn (won by White's exchange sacrifice) is actually a target now, the Bb7+Bg7 battery aims at g2/h1, and the …c5 break gives Black queenside counterplay. White's investment of a piece is suddenly suspect: the kingside attack has stalled and Black's pieces are coordinated for a counterattack on g2.

results.name: Fried Liver Setup
results.pgn: e4 e5 Nf3 Nc6 Bc4 Nf6 Ng5 d5 exd5 Nxd5 Nxf7 Kxf7 Qf3+ Ke6 Nc3 Nb4 O-O c6 d4 Qf6 Qd1 Ke7
results.explanation: This is the Fried Liver Attack — a positional sacrifice, not an immediate-material trap. After Nxf7 Kxf7 Qf3+ Ke6 Nc3, White is DOWN a knight on the board but Black's king is stranded on e6 with no escape squares, no developed pieces, and White's queen + knight + bishop already pointing at the king. The advantage is positional: White's attacking pieces are coordinated and Black's king is hung out to dry. Material recovers gradually through the attack, not in the next two moves. Tell the student: the win here is positional pressure, not a 3-move tactic.

results.name: d4 Push Catching the Bishop
results.pgn: e4 e5 Nf3 Nc6 Bc4 Bc5 d4 Bxd4 Nxd4 Nxd4 Be3 Nc6 Qh5 Qe7 Nc3 Nf6 Qh4 d6 Bg5 Be6
results.explanation: If Black grabs d4 with the bishop, Nxd4 Nxd4 Be3 hits the wandering knight. White's lead in development plus the dark-squared bishop on the long diagonal gives lasting pressure.

results.name: Ng5 f7 Pressure
results.pgn: e4 e5 Nf3 Nc6 Bc4 Nf6 d4 exd4 O-O Nxe4 Re1 d5 Bxd5 Qxd5 Nc3 Qa5 Nxe4 Be6 Neg5 O-O-O Nxe6 fxe6 Rxe6 Bd6
results.explanation: In the mainline with d4, if Black grabs e4 with the knight, Re1 pins it. After Neg5 + Nxe6, the long forced sequence opens the e-file with the rook on the dangerous e6 square — White's lead in development translates into pressure throughout the middlegame.

results.name: Two Knights Bg4 Pin Trick
results.pgn: e4 c6 Nc3 d5 Nf3 Bg4 h3 Bh5 exd5 cxd5 Bb5+ Nc6 g4 Bg6 Ne5 Rc8 d4 e6
results.explanation: When White tries to win the bishop pair forcefully with 4.h3 Bh5 5.g4, Black is happy to give it up: 5…Bg6, then …Nc6, …e6, …Rc8 building up on the c-file. The g4 advance has permanently weakened White's kingside, and the Nb1–Nc3 knight is uncomfortably pinned. Black's prepared, White's pieces aren't — a typical Caro-Kann tempo edge.

results.name: Fantasy Variation e5 Trap
results.pgn: e4 c6 d4 d5 f3 dxe4 fxe4 e5 Nf3 exd4 Bc4 Bb4+
results.explanation: Cautionary tale: the natural-looking 3…dxe4 in the Fantasy is a structural disaster. After fxe4 e5, White has a powerful e4–d4 pawn duo AND the half-open f-file aimed at f7. Black's classical equalisation methods (…Bg4, …Nf6) all run into tactical issues with the f-file pressure. Always play 3…e6 keeping things solid — accept that the Fantasy is the only line where Black has to play defensively.

results.name: Advance Variation Qb6 Fork
results.pgn: e4 c6 d4 d5 e5 Bf5 Nf3 e6 Be2 c5 O-O cxd4 Nxd4
results.explanation: A common Caro-Kann punishment of White's casual Advance play. After Black's standard setup (…c5 + …cxd4 + …Bf5), the …Qb6 fork hits b2 and d4 simultaneously. If White hasn't played Nc3 OR doesn't have an immediate defence ready, the b2-pawn falls. White must play this line very precisely — and most don't.

results.name: Bf5 e6 Bb4 Pin Trap
results.pgn: e4 d5 exd5 Qxd5 Nc3 Qa5 d4 Nf6 Nf3 Bf5 Bd3 e6 O-O
flags: WEAK_TRAP: student only +0 material, no mate (kind=trap, expected ≥ +3 or mate)
STUDENT_NOT_PUNISHER: trap PGN ends with opponent move (last SAN: O-O); student never plays the punishment in this line
results.explanation: A textbook Scandinavian moment — White plays the natural 6.Bd3 attacking your bishop on f5. Instead of trading (which Black usually wants to avoid), Black plays 6...e6 reinforcing the f5-bishop with the e-pawn. Now after 7.O-O, look at the long diagonal: Nc3 is pinned against the e1-king by Qa5 (no bishop blocks the diagonal). The next move …Bb4! piles on the pin. If White doesn't find an exact defence (often Bd2 + a3), the c3-knight falls or White loses the queenside pawn structure to forced …Bxc3+ trades. The trap exploits the e1-h4 diagonal — that's why Qa5 was placed there from move 3.

results.name: c2 Weakness Exploitation
results.pgn: e4 d5 exd5 Qxd5 Nc3 Qa5 d4 Nf6 Nf3 Bf5 Bc4 e6 O-O c6 Re1
results.explanation: After Black's standard setup — Qa5, Bf5, e6, c6 — every Black piece points at White's queenside. The c6-pawn supports a future …b5 expansion; the queen on a5 eyes a2 and e1; the bishop on f5 controls the c2-square (the d3-square is where White's bishop went, but c2 stays under pressure). If White overplays with something like Nh4 trying to trade the bishop, Black redirects with …Bg4 attacking the f3-knight (now without back-up), and the queenside pressure becomes overwhelming. Lesson: in the Scandinavian, your Bf5 is doing TWO jobs at once — kingside development AND queenside c2-pressure.

results.name: Knight Fork Trick
results.pgn: e4 d5 exd5 Qxd5 Nc3 Qa5 d4 Nf6 Bd2 c6 Nf3 Bf5 Bc4 e6 Nd5 Qd8 Nxf6+ gxf6 Qe2 Nd7
results.explanation: White tries to disrupt Black's setup with 8.Nd5 — apparently winning a tempo on the f6-knight. But Black retreats the queen to d8 calmly. After 9.Nxf6+ gxf6 (the only recapture — Black's structure is ugly), Black accepts doubled f-pawns in exchange for the half-open g-file AND the bishop pair (you've kept both your bishops). The …Nd7 reroute heads to f8 or e5 supporting the kingside. Looks like Black is worse — but the bishop pair plus the open g-file gives genuine compensation. Levy's point: don't fear ugly pawns if the dynamic compensation is real.

results.name: Premature c5 Break Punishes White
results.pgn: d4 d5 c4 e6 Nf3 Nf6 Nc3 Be7 Bf4 O-O e3 c5 dxc5 Bxc5 Qc2 Nc6 a3 Qa5 Rd1 Be7 Be2
results.explanation: The Bf4 system as Levy teaches it. After Black plays …c5 (the central equalising break) and the dust settles with …Bxc5, White's natural development with Qc2 + a3 + Rd1 looks solid — but each move loses a tempo. By move 11 (11.Be2) White still hasn't castled and Black has reached a position with full development, the Qa5 well-placed, and the …Be7 redirect prepping the …e5 break. The lesson: in the QGD, White's slight opening advantage evaporates if Black plays the …c5 break at the right moment.

results.name: Nb4-c2 Fork Threat
results.pgn: d4 d5 c4 e6 Nf3 Nf6 Nc3 Be7 Bf4 O-O e3 c5 dxc5 Bxc5 Be2 Nc6 O-O
flags: WEAK_TRAP: student only +0 material, no mate (kind=trap, expected ≥ +3 or mate)
STUDENT_NOT_PUNISHER: trap PGN ends with opponent move (last SAN: O-O); student never plays the punishment in this line
results.explanation: The classic QGD knight tour — after Black plays …c5 + …Bxc5 + …Nc6, the next move on Black's wishlist is …Nb4 forking the queen on c2 (in some lines) AND threatening …Nc2 forking king and rook on a1. White must address this concretely — usually with a3 forcing the knight back to a6 — but every move spent on the knight chase is a move Black gets to improve a different piece. Practical QGD: harass with the knight, develop while the opponent reacts.

results.name: Early dxc4 Pawn Grab
results.pgn: d4 d5 c4 e6 Nf3 Nf6 Nc3 Be7 Bf4 O-O e3 dxc4 Bxc4
results.explanation: Sometimes — but only sometimes — …dxc4 makes sense in the QGD. Specifically: when Black can follow up with …Nd5 attacking the c3-knight AND the bishop on f4, gaining the bishop pair in some lines (after …Nxc3 bxc3). The c-pawn capture loses central tension but gains piece activity. The trade-off: White gets a clean Bxc4 development and the central pawn duo dissolves. Play this only if you've calculated the consequences — most amateurs play …dxc4 reflexively and lose the central battle.

results.name: Knight Fork Trap
results.pgn: e4 e5 Nf3 Nc6 c3 Nf6 d4 Nxe4 d5 Ne7 Nxe5
results.explanation: Black's WORST move after 5.d5 is the natural-looking 5...Ne7 (retreating to safety). But this fails to the simple 6.Nxe5! — White wins the e5-pawn back AND the e4-knight is now hanging (defended only by the Ne7 which is several moves from being able to recapture). After Black tries to extract the e4-knight, White's lead in development plus the extra centre pawn translate into a clean winning position. The lesson: when …Nxe4 is met by d5, Black's ONLY defence is 5...Bc5 (counterattack) — never the passive retreat.

results.name: cxd4 cxd4 Powerful Center
results.pgn: e4 e6 d4 d5 e5 c5 c3 Nc6 Nf3 cxd4 cxd4 Bb4+
results.explanation: When Black plays 5...cxd4 first (before …Qb6), you recapture 6.cxd4 reaching a position where Black tries 6...Bb4+ disrupting your development. Now your simplest answer is 7.Nc3! — accepting the pin temporarily. After 7...Nge7 (or any Black move), 8.Bd3 + 9.O-O completes development. Black wasted a tempo on the bishop check (you'll play a3 later if needed) and your central pawn duo e5+d4 is intact. Not the Milner-Barry's gambit version, but the same strong central play.

results.name: Qb6 Greed Punished
results.pgn: e4 e6 d4 d5 e5 c5 c3 Nc6 Nf3 Qb6 Bd3 cxd4 cxd4 Bd7 O-O Nxd4 Nxd4 Qxd4 Nc3 Qb6
results.explanation: If Black retreats the queen to b6 (instead of staying on d4 to defend), they've thrown away the gambit pawn's value — they have the pawn but White has ALL the development and ALL the tempos. After 10.Nc3 Qb6 the queen is decoyed to a square where she's just a target; White continues 11.Qe2 + Rd1, getting in position to pile on c2 or push e-file aggression. The lesson: when accepting the Milner-Barry pawn, Black's queen MUST stay on d4 dominating the centre — anywhere else is a free tempo for White.

results.name: a6 Bxc6 Doubled Pawns
results.pgn: e4 c5 Nf3 Nc6 Bb5 a6 Bxc6 dxc6 d3 Bg4
results.explanation: When Black plays 3...a6 trying to chase the bishop, you commit with 4.Bxc6! dxc6 (the standard recapture — Black keeps the c-pawn duo) 5.d3 (NOT d4 yet — keep the centre flexible). The position now: Black has doubled c-pawns AND the c-file isn't fully open to compensate. The c6-pawn becomes a permanent weakness — defending it ties up Black's pieces for the entire middlegame. White's plan: develop with Nc3, h3, O-O, then maneuver pieces to attack c6. Black's …Bg4 pin tries to disrupt — you simply play h3 + Bh5 forcing the trade.

results.name: Nd4 Fork Trick Exploited
results.pgn: e4 c5 Nf3 Nc6 Bb5 Nd4 Nxd4 cxd4 O-O a6 Bc4 e6
results.explanation: Black sometimes tries the 'fork trick' with 3...Nd4!? attacking your knight on f3 and threatening to fork the bishop on b5. The trap: you respond 4.Nxd4! cxd4 — Black 'forks' but actually just gives up a pawn for nothing concrete. The d4-pawn is isolated and weak; you castle, develop, and the pawn drops in 10-15 moves. After 5.O-O a6 6.Bc4 e6 you've completed development with the better-placed pieces; Black has a target on d4 that requires defence every move. This 'trick' is a Rossolimo gift.

results.name: Slow Development d4 Push
results.pgn: e4 c5 Nf3 Nc6 Bb5 e6 O-O Nge7 d4 cxd4 Nxd4 a6 Be2 Nxd4 Qxd4 Nc6
results.explanation: When Black develops slowly with 4...Nge7 without immediately challenging the centre, you can play the aggressive d4 break IMMEDIATELY. After 5.d4 cxd4 6.Nxd4 a6 (Black finally kicks the bishop), 7.Be2 (the retreat — bishop stays useful) Nxd4 (Black tries to simplify) 8.Qxd4 Nc6 — you've reached an Open Sicilian-style position but with one MASSIVE difference: Black wasted three tempos (the …Nge7 + …Nxd4 + …Nc6 dance), while you developed harmoniously. The Open Sicilian theory doesn't help Black here because they entered it three tempos behind.

results.name: Berlin Tarrasch Trap
results.pgn: e4 e5 Nf3 Nc6 Bb5 d6 d4 Bd7 Nc3 Nf6 O-O Be7 Re1 O-O
results.explanation: After Black castles too early in the Steinitz/Berlin setup, White wins a piece with 8.Bxc6 Bxc6 9.dxe5 dxe5 10.Qxd8 Raxd8 11.Nxe5 Bxe4 12.Nxe4 Nxe4 13.Nd3 — Black's knight on e4 has no good retreat. A classical 19th-century opening trap documented under Ruy Lopez: Berlin Defense, Tarrasch Trap (ECO C66).

results.name: Greedy c4 Pawn Grab
results.pgn: d4 Nf6 c4 e6 g3 d5 Bg2 dxc4 Nf3 Nc6 O-O Rb8 Nc3 b5 Ne5
flags: WEAK_TRAP: student only -1 material, no mate (kind=trap, expected ≥ +3 or mate)
results.explanation: When Black tries to actually KEEP the c4-pawn (the dream of every amateur 1.d4 player who thought they'd win a pawn), the punishment is brutal. After 5.Nf3 Nc6 6.O-O Rb8 (Black plans …b5 to defend c4) 7.Nc3 b5 8.Ne5! — the knight invades on a key central square, attacks the c6-knight, and prepares Nxb5 forks. Black's queenside pawn structure (b5+c4) is built on sand — every White piece is pointed at it. Within 3-5 moves, Black either loses the c4-pawn back PLUS some structural quality, or loses material to a Bg2-driven attack on the long diagonal.

results.name: Queen Trapped on Diagonal
results.pgn: d4 Nf6 c4 e6 g3 d5 Bg2 Be7 Nf3 O-O O-O dxc4 Qc2 a6 Qxc4 b5 Qc2 Bb7 Bf4 Nd5 Nc3 Nxf4 gxf4 Qd7
flags: WEAK_TRAP: student only +0 material, no mate (kind=trap, expected ≥ +3 or mate)
STUDENT_NOT_PUNISHER: trap PGN ends with opponent move (last SAN: Qd7); student never plays the punishment in this line
results.explanation: A subtle Catalan tactical theme. Black plays the typical setup with …b5 + …Bb7 trying to neutralise the long diagonal. After 11.Bf4 (developing) Nd5 (Black tries to trade knights and bishop pair) 12.Nc3 Nxf4 13.gxf4 — the doubled f-pawns LOOK like a weakness, but they're a strength: the g-file is half-open for the rook AND the Bg2 still owns the diagonal. After 13...Qd7, Black has nominal equality but the white attacking pieces are aimed at the kingside; the next White move (Rg1) sets up the attack.

results.name: Exchange Sac on c1
results.pgn: e4 c5 Nf3 Nc6 d4 cxd4 Nxd4 Nf6 Nc3 e5 Ndb5 d6 Bg5 a6 Na3 b5 Nd5 Be7 Bxf6 Bxf6 c3 Bg5 Nc2 O-O g3
results.explanation: The Sveshnikov's signature tactical theme. After the mainline reaches the standard middlegame, Black's bishop on g5 prepares the thematic exchange sacrifice …Bxc1. After 13.g3 (White prevents …Bxh2+ tactics first), Black has the resource of …Bxc1 in many continuations — sacrificing the dark-squared bishop for the rook. Why? Because Black gets the bishop pair + the powerful e-pawn + dynamic piece play, and the exchange (worth ~2 points) is more than compensated by the position. Magnus has employed this exchange sac at elite level — the lesson: in the Sveshnikov, material count is less important than activity.

results.name: Aggressive d4 Backfire
results.pgn: e4 e5 Nf3 Nc6 Bb5 Nf6 O-O Nxe4 d4 Nd6 Bxc6 dxc6 dxe5 Nf5 Qxd8+ Kxd8 Nc3 Ke8
results.explanation: This is THE Berlin Wall position — when White accepts the forced trade and reaches the queenless endgame, the assessment is roughly equal but Black has the dynamic chances. Walk the sequence: 5...Nxe4 (the only critical move) 6.d4 Nd6 (the knight retreats) 7.Bxc6 dxc6 (recapturing with the d-pawn doubles Black's c-pawns but opens the d-file) 8.dxe5 Nf5 (knight goes to f5 — its only good square) 9.Qxd8+ Kxd8 — kings trade queens. The position now: Black has bishops on c8 and f8 (the trumps), king on d8 walking to e8, and a slightly worse structure. Bishop pair endgames in open positions favour Black.

results.name: Berlin Queen Trade Surprise
results.pgn: e4 e5 Nf3 Nc6 Bb5 Nf6 O-O Nxe4 Re1 Nd6 Nxe5 Be7 Bf1 Nxe5 Rxe5 O-O d4 Bf6 Re1 Nf5 c3 d5
results.explanation: When White tries 5.Re1 (instead of 5.O-O Nxe4 6.d4) to AVOID the Berlin endgame, Black still gets a fine position: 5...Nd6 (saving the knight) 6.Nxe5 Be7 7.Bf1 (the bishop retreats from the Bb5-pin) Nxe5 8.Rxe5 O-O — both sides develop. After 9.d4 Bf6 10.Re1 Nf5 11.c3 d5 — Black's knight on f5 is well-placed, the bishop on f6 is active, and Black has reached an equal middlegame WITHOUT entering the queenless endgame. A flexible alternative when you want to avoid endgame theory.

results.name: Premature e4 Tactics
results.pgn: d4 Nf6 c4 e6 Nc3 d5 Bg5 Be7 e3 O-O Nf3 Nbd7 Bd3 dxc4 Bxc4 c5 O-O cxd4 exd4 Nb6 Bb3 Nbd5
results.explanation: After Bg5 + e3 + Nf3 + Bd3 + cxd4 setup, Black plays the typical ...Nb6 + ...Nbd5 manoeuvre to claim the e4 square. The pin on g5 makes any premature e4 break tactical, and Black's piece activity becomes the dominant theme.

results.name: Bishop Catch with h6-g5
results.pgn: d4 Nf6 c4 e6 Nc3 d5 Bg5 Be7 e3 O-O Nf3 h6 Bh4 b6 cxd5 Nxd5 Bxe7 Qxe7 Nxd5 exd5 Bd3
flags: WEAK_TRAP: student only +0 material, no mate (kind=trap, expected ≥ +3 or mate)
STUDENT_NOT_PUNISHER: trap PGN ends with opponent move (last SAN: Bd3); student never plays the punishment in this line
results.explanation: After Bg5 Be7 e3 h6 Bh4, if White trades on e7 + d5, Black gets the bishop pair and central pressure with ...c5 — White's bishop pair was traded away when the h-pawn was provoked.

results.name: Wrong Attack e5 Fork
results.pgn: e4 c5 Nf3 d6 d4 cxd4 Nxd4 Nf6 Nc3 a6 Be2 e5 Nb3 Be7 O-O O-O f4 b5
results.explanation: After the standard Najdorf English Attack setup, Black breaks with ...b5 to launch queenside counterplay before White's f4-f5 thrust gains momentum.

results.name: Nxe4 Pawn Grab
results.pgn: e4 c5 Nf3 d6 d4 cxd4 Nxd4 Nf6 Nc3 a6 Be2 e5 Nb3 Be7 O-O O-O Be3 Be6 f3 Nbd7
results.explanation: When White is underdeveloped or has loosened the kingside with f3, Black builds the standard Be6 + Nbd7 setup. The ...Nxe4 break later targets f3's weakening — Black's piece coordination gives lasting tactical chances.

results.name: Premature ...d4 Pawn Storm
results.pgn: Nf3 d5 g3 Nf6 Bg2 e6 O-O Be7 d3 O-O Nbd2 d4
results.explanation: When Black plays …d4 closing the centre prematurely (without securing the d-pawn with proper support), they've actually helped you. The d4-pawn isn't attacking anything important AND locks the centre forever. You can now launch the kingside attack with no central counterplay possible — f4 + f5 + Nh2-g4 + h4-h5 — Black has no way to break open the centre to slow you down. The trap exposes the amateur fear of 'losing the centre'; closing it is often worse than abandoning it.

results.name: Early ...c5 Long Diagonal
results.pgn: Nf3 d5 g3 Nf6 Bg2 e6 O-O Be7 d3 O-O Nbd2 c5 e4 dxe4 dxe4
results.explanation: When Black plays …c5 + …dxe4 quickly without ensuring the long diagonal stays closed, the Bg2 becomes devastating. After 7.e4 dxe4 8.dxe4, the diagonal a8-h1 is now open with NO Black piece to block it. The Bg2 stares at b7 AND eyes future e4-e5 advances. Black's pieces lack coordination because they haven't been developed yet, while White's pieces are all ready. This is why the KIA's opening principle ('setup before plan') matters: Black needs a setup BEFORE engaging, but the KIA is hard to engage.

results.name: Misplaced Queen ...Qh4
results.pgn: e4 e5 Nf3 Nc6 d4 exd4 Nxd4 Qh4 Nb5 Qxe4+ Be2
results.explanation: The same 4...Qh4 trap that punishes Naroditsky's Scotch — White's response is 5.Nb5! threatening Nxc7+ AND Nd6+, forcing 5...Qxe4+ 6.Be2 (calmly developing). Black has bought ONE pawn for the next 10 moves of misery; meanwhile Hikaru is calculating moves 3 ahead and Black hasn't completed development yet. The same trap works in bullet because the queen-wandering pattern is forced — Black can't think their way out.

results.name: ...Qb6 Meets Qb3 Counter
results.pgn: d4 d5 Nf3 Nf6 Bf4 c5 e3 Nc6 c3 Qb6 Qb3 c4 Qc2 Bf5 Qc1 e6 Nbd2
results.explanation: The standard …Qb6 → Qb3 → …c4 → Qc2 → …Bf5 → Qc1 sequence. Black thinks they're pressuring b2 and winning a tempo; in reality, White's queen dance forces Black to commit pieces (Qc2 + Bf5 + Qc1 brings the queen back to a safe square AND Black has spent two tempos with the queen and Bf5). The position transposes into a normal London. The trap exposes the amateur's belief that hitting b2 wins material — it doesn't, because White has prepared resources.

results.name: ...Nh5 Bishop Hunt Backfires
results.pgn: d4 d5 Nf3 Nf6 Bf4 c5 e3 Nc6 c3 Nh5 Bg5 h6 Bh4 g5 Bg3 Nxg3 hxg3
results.explanation: When Black plays …Nh5 trying to harass the f4-bishop, the London teacher's response is the bishop tour: 5...Nh5 6.Bg5 h6 7.Bh4 g5 8.Bg3 Nxg3 9.hxg3 — Black has traded the f6-knight for your dark-squared bishop. Material is even (minor piece for minor piece). The structural picture: White has doubled g-pawns (g2+g3) — visually ugly but actually compensated by the OPEN h-file for your rook lift. Black has FORCED their own kingside weakening (…h6+…g5) to win the trade. With your rook on h1 staring down the open file at Black's vulnerable kingside, you're attacking immediately while Black struggles to coordinate. The 'ugly' doubled g-pawns are the price of the attacking lane.

results.name: Najdorf e6 Pin Trick
results.pgn: e4 c5 Nf3 d6 d4 cxd4 Nxd4 Nf6 Nc3 a6 Be3 e5 Nb3 Be6 f3 Be7 Qd2 O-O O-O-O b5 g4 b4 Nd5 Bxd5 exd5 a5 Kb1 a4 Nc1
results.explanation: The English Attack's signature counterattack. After both sides have castled opposite ways, Black plays …b5 + …b4 forcing White's knight (Nc3) to move. White's only good square is Nd5 — but Black responds 12...Bxd5 13.exd5 (the structure now favours Black — White's pawn duo is gone). Black continues 13...a5 14.Kb1 a4 — the pawns roll toward White's king. White's knight retreats to c1 (clumsy) and Black's attack arrives faster than White's. The queenside race won; the kingside attack never gets started.

results.name: Premature d5 and ...c6 Break
results.pgn: d4 Nf6 c4 g6 Nc3 Bg7 e4 d6 Nf3 O-O Be2 e5 d5 a5 Bg5 h6 Bh4 Na6 Nd2 Qe8 O-O Nh7 a3
results.explanation: Black plays the slower KID setup with …a5 + …Na6 (a more positional KID) instead of the immediate attack. The Na6 + Nh7 + Qe8 manoeuvre is the slow build-up to …f5 + …Nf6 reroute — Black accepts that the attack will take longer but ensures the queenside is defended (…a5 prevents White's b4 break). A more strategic version of the KID for positional players.

results.name: Kingside ...f5 Pawn Storm
results.pgn: d4 Nf6 c4 g6 Nc3 Bg7 e4 d6 Nf3 O-O Be2 e5 O-O Nc6 d5 Ne7 Ne1 Nd7 Nd3 f5 f3 f4 g4
results.explanation: The dream KID position. After …f5 → f4, White tries to prevent the kingside collapse with 12.g4 — but Black has been waiting for this. After 12.g4, the g-file is permanently weakened, the f-pawn is locked in place, and Black plays …h5 next to crack the g-file open. The kingside attack now has open files for the rooks (h-file after …h5 hxg4 hxg5 or similar). The pawn structure is sealed; Black's attack arrives first by 2-3 tempos.

results.name: Early a3 Doubles Pawns
results.pgn: d4 Nf6 c4 e6 Nc3 Bb4 a3 Bxc3+ bxc3 O-O f3 d5 cxd5 exd5 e3 c5 Bd3 Nc6 Ne2 Re8 O-O
results.explanation: When White plays the Sämisch 4.a3 forcing …Bxc3+ 5.bxc3 — White accepts the structural concession (doubled c-pawns) for the bishop pair. Black exploits the weakness IMMEDIATELY: 5...O-O 6.f3 d5 7.cxd5 exd5 — Black has the better pawn structure AND the open e-file. After 8.e3 c5 (attacking the c3-pawn) 9.Bd3 Nc6 10.Ne2 Re8 11.O-O — Black has placed every piece on its best square, while White's structural problems are permanent.

results.name: ...c5 Timing Trick
results.pgn: d4 Nf6 c4 e6 Nc3 Bb4 Qc2 O-O a3 Bxc3+ Qxc3 d5 Nf3 dxc4 Qxc4 b6 Bg5 Ba6 Qa4 c5 dxc5 bxc5 Rd1 Qb6
results.explanation: The Nimzo's tactical timing of the …c5 break. After the standard Capablanca-style queen-recapture sequence, Black plays the …c5 break at the EXACT moment when White's queen is awkwardly placed on a4. After 12...c5! 13.dxc5 bxc5 14.Rd1 Qb6 — Black has mobilised the queenside majority AND the d4-pawn structure is gone, leaving Black with mobile c-pawns and active pieces. The position favours Black's dynamic resources.

results.name: Premature e5 Trap
results.pgn: d4 Nf6 c4 c5 d5 b5 cxb5 a6 bxa6 Bxa6 Nc3 d6 e4 Bxf1 Kxf1 g6 g3 Bg7 Kg2 O-O Nf3 Nbd7 Re1 Qa5
results.explanation: After the standard king-walk setup, Black's …Qa5 + …Rfb8 (coming next) coordinates the queenside attack. The …Qa5 has THREE jobs: (1) eyes a4 and b4 putting pressure on White's queenside, (2) supports the …Rb8 → …Rb4 rook lift, (3) covers the …Bxc3 idea sacrificing the bishop for the c3-knight if White's king becomes exposed. With every Black piece pointed at the queenside, White's position becomes permanently defensive.

results.name: Premature ...d4 Closing Center
results.pgn: c4 e5 Nc3 Nf6 Nf3 Nc6 g3 d5 cxd5 Nxd5 Bg2 Nb6 O-O Be7 d3 O-O Be3 f5 Rc1 Be6 Na4 Nxa4
flags: INVERTED_MATERIAL: student down 3 in material (kind=mistake, expected ≥ 0)
results.explanation: Black plays the f5 expansion while White maneuvers Na4 — when Black trades the knight, White claims dark-square pressure with Bc5 + Bg2 fully active.

results.name: Marshall ...d5 Prevented by h3
results.pgn: e4 e5 Nf3 Nc6 Bb5 a6 Ba4 Nf6 O-O Be7 Re1 b5 Bb3 O-O h3 d5 exd5 Nxd5 Nxe5 Nxe5 Rxe5
results.explanation: When White plays 8.h3 BEFORE c3, the move-order rules out the Marshall. After 8...d5 (Black tries anyway) 9.exd5 Nxd5 10.Nxe5 Nxe5 11.Rxe5 — White is up a pawn AND has an extra tempo (h3 was a useful waiting move). Black's compensation is missing because the …Bd6/…Qh4 setup is one tempo too slow. White consolidates with d4 + Be3 + Nd2 and the extra pawn wins the endgame.

results.name: Premature ...d5 Without h3
results.pgn: e4 e5 Nf3 Nc6 Bb5 a6 Ba4 Nf6 O-O Be7 Re1 b5 Bb3 O-O c3 d5 exd5 Nxd5 Nxe5 Nxe5 Rxe5 c6 d4 Bd6 Re1 Qh4 g3 Qh3 Be3 Bg4
results.explanation: If you face the full Marshall and have prepared the modern defence (Be3 + Bg4 line), you reach the position above. After Black's …Bg4 trying to attack via the f3 square, White plays Nd2 + Bf1 (defending h2) + Bxh2 — refuting the attack. The deep theoretical line goes 20+ moves with both sides forced to play exactly. Caruana's depth in this position is unmatched; he's the only player who can confidently accept the Marshall and play for a win as White.

results.name: ...exd4 cxd4 Center Steamroll
results.pgn: e4 e5 Nf3 Nc6 Bc4 Bc5 c3 Nf6 d4 exd4 cxd4 Bb4+ Bd2 Bxd2+ Nbxd2 d5 exd5 Nxd5 O-O O-O
results.explanation: When Black accepts the standard sequence …exd4 cxd4 …Bb4+ …Bxd2+ …Nbxd2 …d5 …Nxd5, you reach the IQP position with both sides castled. This is the textbook Caruana set-up. From here, the plan is automatic: Qb3 (pressure on b7), Rfe1 (centralising), Nb3 (the d2-knight reroutes to support d4), Bd3 (the c4-bishop reroutes to aim at h7). Every Caruana win in this line follows the same blueprint: build pressure, trade ONLY when ahead, win the endgame.

results.name: ...a6 Bishop Pair Advantage
results.pgn: e4 e5 Nf3 Nc6 Bb5 Nf6 d3 a6 Bxc6 dxc6
results.explanation: When Black plays …a6 (the natural Ruy Lopez reaction), Caruana's response is the immediate 5.Bxc6! — happily trading the bishop. The …dxc6 recapture gives Black the doubled c-pawns; White keeps the bishop pair AND has saved the typical Ba4 retreat tempo. The permanent structural damage on the queenside is the trap's payoff: Black has to defend c6+c7 for the entire rest of the game.

results.name: Premature d4 and ...Qh4 Pressure
results.pgn: e4 e5 Nf3 Nf6 Nxe5 d6 Nf3 Nxe4 d4 d5 Bd3 Bd6 O-O O-O c4 c6 Nc3 Nxc3 bxc3 dxc4 Bxc4 Bg4 Rb1 b5 Bd3 Nd7
results.explanation: When White plays d4 + c4 + Nc3 aiming for an active middlegame, Black responds calmly with mirror development: …Bd6 (active bishop) + …O-O + …c6 (defending d5). After 9.Nxc3 10.bxc3 dxc4 (Black trades) 11.Bxc4 Bg4 (the standard pin) 12.Rb1 b5 (Black expands queenside) 13.Bd3 Nd7 — the dust settles into a position where Black has solid development and active pieces. The forced exchanges have neutralised White's opening edge.

results.name: Nf3 Retreat Strong Center
results.pgn: e4 e5 Nf3 Nf6 Nxe5 d6 Nf3 Nxe4 d4 d5 Bd3 Nc6 O-O Be7 c4 Nb4 Be2 O-O Nc3 Bf5 a3 Nxc3 bxc3 Nc6 Re1 Re8 Bf4
results.explanation: After Black's standard equalising sequence reaches the position, both sides develop along clear plans: White completes with 14.Re1 Re8 15.Bf4 attacking c7 (indirectly), Black plays the standard …Nc6 reroute back. The position is dead-equal but the better-prepared side wins. Caruana wins these as Black because his preparation extends 5-10 moves deeper than most opponents'.

results.name: Premature e4 Punished
results.pgn: d4 Nf6 c4 e6 Nc3 d5 Nf3 Be7 Bf4 O-O e3 Nbd7 Be2 c5 dxc5 Nxc5 O-O dxc4 Bxc4 a6
results.explanation: After the standard QGD development reaches the position 9.O-O dxc4 10.Bxc4 a6, if White tries the premature e4 push, Black has …b5 + …Bb7 ready. The b5-pawn attacks the c4-bishop AND defends c6; the Bb7 hits e4. White's e4 push, far from giving a central advantage, hangs to Black's coordinated queenside attack.

results.name: Lasker Defense ...Ne4 Exchange
results.pgn: d4 Nf6 c4 e6 Nc3 d5 Bg5 Be7 e3 O-O Nf3 h6 Bh4 Ne4 Bxe7 Qxe7 cxd5 Nxc3 bxc3 exd5 Qb3 Rd8 c4 dxc4 Bxc4 Nc6 O-O Na5
results.explanation: Emanuel Lasker's classic equalising idea, still played a century later. After 7.Bh4 (White avoids the Exchange) Ne4! — the knight reaches a centralised square AND threatens the dark-squared bishop trade. 8.Bxe7 Qxe7 9.cxd5 Nxc3 10.bxc3 exd5 — Black has saddled White with doubled c-pawns AND traded off the active dark-squared bishop. The 13...Na5! manoeuvre attacks c4 directly, forcing White to choose between defending or trading off the bishop pair. Caruana's preparation here ensures the line is essentially forced equality.

results.name: f3 Overextension Punished
results.pgn: d4 Nf6 c4 e6 Nc3 Bb4 f3 d5 a3 Bxc3+ bxc3 c5 cxd5 exd5 e3 O-O Bd3 b6 Ne2 Ba6 Bxa6 Nxa6 O-O Nc7 Ng3 Re8 Qd3 cxd4
results.explanation: When White plays the aggressive 4.f3 (Sämisch) supporting e4, Black punishes the kingside weakness immediately: 4...d5 5.a3 Bxc3+ 6.bxc3 (doubled c-pawns AND weakened kingside) c5 7.cxd5 exd5 — Black has the IQP but White's structural problems are worse. After 13...cxd4 — White's d4-pawn falls, the doubled c-pawns become decisive weaknesses, Black's pieces dominate. The Sämisch is sharp but unsound against accurate play.

results.name: ...c5 Catches White Overextended
results.pgn: d4 Nf6 c4 e6 Nc3 Bb4 Qc2 O-O a3 Bxc3+ Qxc3 d5 Nf3 c5 dxc5
results.explanation: The Nimzo's signature tactical idea. After Black's standard development reaches the position, …c5 hits the d4-pawn at the EXACT moment White's queen is committed to c3. After 8.dxc5, Black plays …dxc4 + …Nbd7 + …Nxc5 recovering the pawn with the more active piece structure. The d4-pawn that White seemed to control is gone; the structural game now favours Black's coordinated pieces.

results.name: Pawn Grab ...dxc4 Cannot Hold
results.pgn: d4 Nf6 c4 e6 g3 d5 Bg2 dxc4 Nf3 a6 O-O Nc6 e3 Bd7 Qe2 b5 a4 Rb8
results.explanation: When Black tries to actually KEEP the c4-pawn (the dream of every amateur 1.d4 player), the punishment is swift. After 4...dxc4 5.Nf3 a6 (preparing …b5) 6.O-O Nc6 7.e3 Bd7 8.Qe2 b5 9.a4! Rb8 — White's a4 push undermines the b5-pawn structure. The c-pawns are about to fall back to White, AND the queenside pawn structure is wrecked. The Catalan bishop on g2 plus the open files compensate for any temporary material deficit.

results.name: Bg2 Diagonal Wins Material
results.pgn: d4 Nf6 c4 e6 g3 d5 Bg2 Be7 Nf3 O-O O-O dxc4 Qc2 a6 a4 Bd7 Qxc4 Bc6 Bf4 Nbd7 Nc3 Nb6 Qd3 Nbd5
flags: WEAK_TRAP: student only +0 material, no mate (kind=trap, expected ≥ +3 or mate)
STUDENT_NOT_PUNISHER: trap PGN ends with opponent move (last SAN: Nbd5); student never plays the punishment in this line
results.explanation: The Catalan's long-term squeeze. After the standard pawn recovery, White's pieces all aim at Black's queenside: the Bg2 hits a8, the Qd3 swings between centre and kingside, the Nc3 supports central breaks. When Black plays …Nbd5 trying to challenge the centre, White responds with Nxd5 + Qxd5 trading pieces. The resulting endgame is structurally balanced but Caruana's bishop pair plus active queen wins the technical phase.

results.name: ...Nd6 Punishes Avoiding Endgame
results.pgn: e4 e5 Nf3 Nc6 Bb5 Nf6 O-O Nxe4 d4 Nd6 Bxc6 dxc6 dxe5 Nf5 Qxd8+ Kxd8 Nc3 Ke8 h3 Be7 Bf4 Be6 Rad1 Rd8
results.explanation: The standard Berlin Endgame setup with rook development. After 12.Rad1 (White centralises the rook) Rd8 — Black mirrors with …Rd8 challenging the d-file. Black's plan from here: walk the king to f7 (via …Ke8-…Kd7-…Kc7 if needed), eventually break with …c5 opening the position. The bishop pair plus accurate technique gives Black equality and the practical winning chances in long endgames.

results.name: Bishop Pair in Berlin Wall
results.pgn: e4 e5 Nf3 Nc6 Bb5 Nf6 O-O Nxe4 d4 Nd6 Bxc6 dxc6 dxe5 Nf5 Qxd8+ Kxd8 Nc3 Ke8 h3 Be7 Bf4 Be6 Rad1
results.explanation: The Berlin Wall's structural complement to the bishop pair. After 12.Rad1, White has centralised the rooks and seems active — but Black's two bishops (Be6 + Be7, soon to redirect) plus the open c-file create long-term equality. The position is balanced; the better technique wins. Caruana's record from this position is approximately 50% as Black at the elite level — meaning he's NEVER worse, just sometimes equal.

results.name: Nxe4 Grab Punished by d5
results.pgn: e4 e5 Nf3 Nc6 Bc4 Bc5 c3 Nf6 d4 Nxe4
flags: WEAK_TRAP: student only -1 material, no mate (kind=trap, expected ≥ +3 or mate)
STUDENT_NOT_PUNISHER: trap PGN ends with opponent move (last SAN: Nxe4); student never plays the punishment in this line
results.explanation: When Black greedily grabs e4 with the knight (instead of the principled 5...exd4), White plays 6.d5! immediately. The d5-pawn forks the c6-knight (which has to move) AND attacks Black's bishop on c5 indirectly. After 6...Ne7 (the only square — Nb8 wastes more tempo) 7.dxc6 — White wins material AND keeps the central pawn duo. Classic Italian punishment for amateurs who 'just grab the pawn'.

results.name: exd4 Center Steamroll
results.pgn: e4 e5 Nf3 Nc6 Bc4 Bc5 c3 Nf6 d4 exd4 cxd4
results.explanation: After the standard 5.d4 exd4 6.cxd4, White has the central pawn duo c-d-e all in the center. Black's options are limited: 6...Bb4+ (forcing the trade — main line), 6...Bb6 (passive), or 6...d5 (challenging the centre with structural concessions). Each move is concrete; Firouzja's preparation typically reaches move 12-15 in any of these branches with the better-placed pieces.

results.name: bxc6 Structural Disaster
results.pgn: e4 c5 Nf3 Nc6 Bb5 g6 Bxc6 bxc6 d3 Bg7 O-O Nf6 Re1 O-O
results.explanation: When Black plays 4...bxc6 (instead of the correct …dxc6), the structure is permanently worse: isolated a-pawn AND doubled c-pawns. After 5.d3 Bg7 6.O-O Nf6 7.Re1 O-O — White has a long-term plan to attack on the queenside (where Black is structurally weak) AND target c6/c7 with rooks and knights. The bishop pair compensation Black has doesn't match the structural permanent damage.

results.name: a6 Bxc6 Structural Edge
results.pgn: e4 c5 Nf3 Nc6 Bb5 a6 Bxc6 dxc6 d3
results.explanation: When Black plays the natural 3...a6 (kicking the bishop), Firouzja commits with 4.Bxc6! dxc6 (forced — Black takes back keeping the c-pawn intact) 5.d3 — same doubled-pawn structure reached via different move order. The …a6 commitment is now wasted because the bishop is gone; meanwhile White has a clear plan to attack the queenside pawns over the next 30 moves.

results.name: exf4 Opens the f-file
results.pgn: e4 e5 Nc3 Nf6 Bc4 Bc5 d3 d6 f4
results.explanation: When Black captures 5...exf4 (the natural pawn-grab response to your f4 push), you recapture with Bxf4 (NOT Bxh6 if the f-pawn went forward without the knight being on f6). The open f-file becomes White's attacking lane — Rf1 lifts to f3 or f5, the Bc4 still aims at f7, and the queen joins via Qe2 or Qf3. Three pieces aimed at f7; the attack often crashes through in 6-8 moves.

results.name: Bg4 Pin Broken by f5
results.pgn: e4 e5 Nc3 Nf6 Bc4 Bc5 d3 d6 f4
results.explanation: When Black plays …Bg4 trying to pin the f3-knight (in lines where Nf3 has been played), the Vienna's response is 6.f5! breaking the pin from the wrong side. The f-pawn attacks Black's e5-pawn AND blocks the diagonal between the Bg4 and the f3-knight. The bishop is forced to retreat, you've gained a tempo AND space.

results.name: Bc4 e6 Nxe4 Trick
results.pgn: e4 c5 Nf3 d6 d4 cxd4 Nxd4 Nf6 Nc3 a6 Bc4 e6 Bb3 b5 O-O Be7 Qf3 Qc7 Qg3
results.explanation: When White plays the Fischer Attack with 6.Bc4 (Bobby Fischer's pet weapon from his championship era), Black needs the CRITICAL move order: 6...e6 BEFORE …b5 (otherwise Bxe6! is winning). After the standard 7.Bb3 b5 8.O-O Be7 9.Qf3 Qc7, White's natural 10.Qg3 (preparing the kingside attack) misplaces the queen — Black is ready for tactical resources like …Nxe4 + …d5 cracking open the centre while White's queen is on the wrong side. Pure Najdorf tactical sharpness.

results.name: Overextended g4 Punished
results.pgn: e4 c5 Nf3 d6 d4 cxd4 Nxd4 Nf6 Nc3 a6 Be3 e5 Nb3 Be7 f3 Be6 Qd2 Nbd7 g4 b5 g5 Nh5 O-O-O Nb6 Nd5 Bxd5 exd5
results.explanation: When White pushes g4 too aggressively (before O-O-O), Black strikes back with 8...b5! starting the counterattack. After 9.g5 (continuing the kingside attack) Nh5 (knight reroutes to a useful square) 10.O-O-O Nb6 (the second knight joins the queenside attack) 11.Nd5! (White's typical tactical resource) Bxd5 12.exd5 — White has the structural pluses but Black's pieces are aimed at the king on c1. The …Nxe4 and …b4 tactics arrive in 3-5 moves; the game is decided by whichever attack lands first.

results.name: Center Overextension Punished by c5
results.pgn: d4 Nf6 c4 g6 Nc3 d5 cxd5 Nxd5 e4 Nxc3 bxc3 Bg7 Bc4 O-O Ne2 c5 O-O Nc6 Be3 cxd4 cxd4 Bg4 f3 Na5 Bd3 Be6
results.explanation: Classic Grünfeld punishment for the Bc4+Ne2 setup. When White over-extends with the big e4+d4 centre AND the Bc4 development, Black plays the textbook 11...c5 (challenging d4) and after the 12.O-O Nc6 13.Be3 cxd4 14.cxd4 trades, Black's pieces are perfectly placed (Bg4 pinning, Na5 attacking c4-bishop) while White's centre is exposed. The …Bxa1 exchange-sacrifice motif is always lurking in the air.

results.name: Nxc3 Qa5+ Exchange Trick
results.pgn: d4 Nf6 c4 g6 Nc3 d5 cxd5 Nxd5 e4 Nxc3 bxc3 Bg7 Qa4+ Nd7 Be3 O-O
results.explanation: The Qa4+ sideline (some Black players use Qa5+ in similar positions). After 6.bxc3 Bg7 7.Qa4+ Nd7 (Black blocks with the queenside knight) 8.Be3 O-O — Black has completed development with the standard plan of targeting the doubled c-pawns. The Qa4 was meant to disrupt Black's development but it loses a tempo when …Nd7 calmly blocks AND develops at the same time.

results.name: Qh5 Kingside Attack Refuted
results.pgn: e4 e6 d4 d5 Nc3 Nf6 Bg5 dxe4 Nxe4 Be7 Bxf6 gxf6 Nf3 Bd7 Nc3
results.explanation: After Black's Burn Variation setup (with the doubled f-pawns), White sometimes tries to exploit the apparent kingside weakness with Qh5-attacks. Black's response: calm development with …Bd7 (preparing …Bc6 fianchetto via this square) + queenside castling. The bishop pair compensates for the structural concession; Black's king is safer than it looks.

results.name: Bishop Pair Compensation
results.pgn: e4 e6 d4 d5 Nc3 Nf6 Bg5 dxe4 Nxe4 Be7 Bxf6 gxf6 Nf3 b6 Bc4 Bb7 Qe2 Nd7 O-O-O
results.explanation: The full Burn position with opposite-side castling. Despite the doubled f-pawns, Black's two bishops (Be7 + Bb7) are powerful in the open position. The Bb7 + half-open g-file for the …Rg8 lift creates real attacking chances against the white king on c1. The …c5 break is coming next move; the position is dynamically balanced with chances for both sides.

results.name: Nxe4 Pawn Grab Refuted
results.pgn: e4 e5 Nf3 Nc6 Bb5 a6 Ba4 Nf6 O-O Nxe4 d4 b5 Bb3 d5 dxe5 Be6 c3 Be7 Re1 Nc5
results.explanation: The Open Ruy Lopez where Black grabs the e-pawn. White's response is the standard 6.d4 challenging the centre AND 8.dxe5 reaching the structurally favourable position. After 9.c3 (supporting the centre) Be7 10.Re1 Nc5 — Black has won a pawn but White's e5-pawn cramps Black's pieces, the c2-bishop aims at h7, and the e-file is open for the rook. The pawn isn't worth the time White has gained.

results.name: Open Tarrasch Trap
results.pgn: e4 e5 Nf3 Nc6 Bb5 a6 Ba4 Nf6 O-O Nxe4 d4 b5 Bb3 d5 dxe5 Be6 c3 Be7 Re1 O-O Nd4
flags: WEAK_TRAP: student only +0 material, no mate (kind=trap, expected ≥ +3 or mate)
results.explanation: The Open Tarrasch Trap (ECO C83) — named after Siegbert Tarrasch who first analyzed the position. In the Open Spanish, Black's 9...Be7 (instead of the correct 9...Bc5) creates a positional weakness on the f7-square. After 11.Nd4! White threatens 12.Nxe6 + 13.Rxe4 winning material — Black must defend with extreme precision. A classic 19th-century opening trap that still catches amateurs in the engine era.

results.name: Premature c5 Punished by b6
results.pgn: d4 Nf6 c4 g6 Nc3 Bg7 e4 d6 Nf3 O-O Be2 e5 O-O Nc6 d5 Ne7 c5
results.explanation: If White pushes 9.c5 prematurely (before getting the b4 push ready), Black has the textbook KID counter: 9...Ne8 (the knight evacuates to support …f5) followed by 10.cxd6 cxd6 11.b4 b6! — Black breaks the queenside chain at exactly the right moment. White's …c5 push collapses, and Black retains the …f5 break for the kingside attack. The KID's flexibility AT ITS BEST.

results.name: f5-f4 Kingside Storm
results.pgn: d4 Nf6 c4 g6 Nc3 Bg7 e4 d6 Nf3 O-O Be2 e5 O-O Nc6 d5 Ne7 Ne1 Nd7 f3 f5 Be3 f4 Bf2 g5 Nd3 Ng6 c5
results.explanation: The full Mar del Plata kingside storm. After Black launches …f5-f4-g5, White's queenside attack with 13.Nd3 (reroutes to support c5) and 14.c5 races against Black's …Ng6 + …Rf6 + …Qe8-h5 attacking buildup. The position is opposite-side castled middlegame at maximum tension — both sides must calculate every move concretely. Firouzja's win-rate in this position at elite level is one of the highest in the KID.

results.name: c4 Grab and Bg2 Recovery
results.pgn: d4 Nf6 c4 e6 g3 d5 Bg2 dxc4 Nf3 a6 O-O Nc6 e3 Bd7 Qe2 b5 a4 Rb8 axb5 axb5
results.explanation: When Black tries to KEEP the c4-pawn (greedy amateur dream), you punish with a4 undermining. After 9.a4 Rb8 10.axb5 axb5 — Black's queenside is wrecked: the open a-file is yours, the b-pawn is weak, and the Bg2 fires down the long diagonal at the now-exposed pieces. The c4-pawn that Black 'won' becomes a target instead of an asset.

results.name: Long Diagonal Skewer
results.pgn: d4 Nf6 c4 e6 g3 d5 Bg2 Be7 Nf3 O-O O-O dxc4 Qc2 a6 a4 Bd7 Qxc4 Bc6 Bf4
flags: WEAK_TRAP: student only +0 material, no mate (kind=trap, expected ≥ +3 or mate)
results.explanation: The standard Catalan recovery sequence with Dubov's preferred Bf4 development. After 9.Bf4 (active bishop, eyes c7) — both bishops are aimed at Black's queenside. The …Bc6 trade attempt is met by the standard Catalan plan of Nbd2 + Rfc1 + Bb2 piling up on the long diagonal. White's structural pressure is permanent.

results.name: Marshall Attack Prevented
results.pgn: e4 e5 Nf3 Nc6 Bb5 a6 Ba4 Nf6 O-O Be7 Re1 b5 Bb3 O-O h3 d5 exd5 Nxd5 Nxe5 Nxe5 Rxe5
results.explanation: The h3 move order prevents the Marshall Attack. If Black tries d5 anyway, White's extra tempo means the attack fizzles — White consolidates the extra pawn.

results.name: Premature d5 Loses a Pawn
results.pgn: e4 e5 Nf3 Nc6 Bb5 a6 Ba4 Nf6 O-O Be7 Re1 b5 Bb3 O-O h3 Bb7 d3 d5 exd5 Nxd5 Nxe5 Nxe5 Rxe5
flags: WEAK_TRAP: student only +1 material, no mate (kind=trap, expected ≥ +3 or mate)
results.explanation: If Black plays d5 prematurely in the Anti-Marshall structure, exd5 followed by Nxe5 + Rxe5 wins a pawn cleanly. White's pieces are better coordinated and the extra pawn tells.

results.name: e5 Space Advantage After c5
results.pgn: e4 e6 d4 d5 Nd2 Nf6 e5 Nfd7 Bd3 c5 c3 Nc6 Ne2 cxd4 cxd4 Qb6 Nf3 f6 exf6 Nxf6 O-O Bd6 Nc3 O-O Bg5
results.explanation: After Black plays c5, White gets e5 gaining a big space advantage. Black must spend time breaking with f6 while White develops freely and targets the kingside.

results.name: dxe4 Nxe4 Central Domination
results.pgn: e4 e6 d4 d5 Nd2 dxe4 Nxe4 Nd7 Nf3 Ngf6 Nxf6+ Nxf6 Bd3 Be7 O-O O-O Qe2 c5
results.explanation: If Black captures dxe4, White recaptures Nxe4 and dominates the center. After completed development on both sides, White's knight outpost potential plus open piece play gives a clear positional edge.

results.name: Nd5 Nxd5 Central Control
results.pgn: e4 c5 Nf3 Nc6 d4 cxd4 Nxd4 Nf6 Nc3 e5 Ndb5 d6 Bg5 a6 Na3 b5 Nd5 Be7 Bxf6 Bxf6 c3 O-O Nc2 Bg5 Nce3
results.explanation: When White plays Nd5, Black accepts the trade — after Bxf6 Bxf6 + c3 + Nc2 + Nce3, both sides maneuver in the Sveshnikov main line where Black's bishop pair counterbalances White's central control.

results.name: a6-b5 Knight Trap
results.pgn: e4 c5 Nf3 Nc6 d4 cxd4 Nxd4 Nf6 Nc3 e5 Ndb5 d6 Bg5 a6 Na3 b5 Bxf6 gxf6 Nd5 Bg7 Bd3 Ne7 Nxe7 Qxe7 c3 f5 Nc2 O-O O-O f4
results.explanation: The a6 and b5 expansion chases White's knight from b5 to the awkward a3 square. Black gets queenside space and the knight takes several moves to reroute, giving Black the initiative.

results.name: dxc5 Active Pieces Compensate
results.pgn: d4 d5 c4 e6 Nc3 c5 cxd5 exd5 Nf3 Nc6 g3 Nf6 Bg2 Be7 O-O O-O dxc5 Bxc5 Bg5 d4 Ne4 Be7 Bxf6 Bxf6
results.explanation: When White captures dxc5, Black gets active piece play compensating for the IQP. After the Ne4 + bishop trades, Black's open lines and dynamic pieces create lasting tactical chances.

results.name: Nc6 d4 Pressure
results.pgn: d4 d5 c4 e6 Nc3 c5 cxd5 exd5 Nf3 Nc6 g3 Nf6 Bg2 Be7 O-O O-O Bg5 cxd4 Nxd4 h6 Be3 Re8 Qb3 Na5 Qc2 Bg4
results.explanation: Black develops Nc6 and puts pressure on d4. After cxd4 Nxd4, the IQP disappears and Black gets active play with Na5 + Bg4 targeting White's slightly awkward piece placement.

results.name: e4-e5 Overextension Punished
results.pgn: d4 Nf6 c4 c5 d5 e6 Nc3 exd5 cxd5 d6 e4 g6 Nf3 Bg7 Be2 O-O O-O Re8 Nd2 Nbd7 f4
results.explanation: When White pushes e4-e5 too fast, Black gets the f6 break opening lines against the overextended center. The Bg7 comes alive and Black's counterplay is strong.

results.name: b5 Queenside Expansion
results.pgn: d4 Nf6 c4 c5 d5 e6 Nc3 exd5 cxd5 d6 e4 g6 Nf3 Bg7 Be2 O-O O-O Re8 Nd2 Na6 f3 Nc7 a4 b5
results.explanation: Black surprises with the b5 expansion. The open b-file and active pieces give Black strong compensation and practical chances for a queenside attack.

results.name: Nxe4 Grab Met by Qf3
results.pgn: e4 e5 Nf3 Nc6 d4 exd4 Nxd4 Nf6 Nc3 Bb4 Nxc6 bxc6 Bd3
results.explanation: Black grabs the e4 pawn with ...Nxe4 but White plays Qf3 attacking the knight and f7. Black is forced into an awkward defense and White recovers the material with advantage.

results.name: Bc5 Be3 Pin
results.pgn: e4 e5 Nf3 Nc6 d4 exd4 Nxd4 Bc5 Be3 Bxd4 Bxd4 Nf6 Nc3 O-O
results.explanation: After Bc5, White plays Be3 creating a pin on the d4 diagonal. If Black trades with Bxd4 Bxd4, White's dark-squared bishop dominates and the kingside attack builds quickly.

results.name: e4 fxe4 Favorable Opening
results.pgn: d4 f5 g3 Nf6 Bg2 g6 Nf3 Bg7 O-O O-O c4 d6 Nc3 e5
results.explanation: White plays e4 trying to blow open the center but after fxe4, Black gets a favorable position. The e5 push completes Black's central setup with the long-diagonal Bg7 ready for action.

results.name: Stonewall d5 Fortress
results.pgn: d4 f5 g3 Nf6 Bg2 e6 Nf3 d5 O-O Bd6 c4 c6 b3 Qe7 Bb2 O-O Nbd2 b6 Ne5 Bb7 Nd3 Nbd7 Nf4
results.explanation: The Stonewall setup with d5-e6-f5 creates an impenetrable fortress in the center. After Nf4 White struggles to break through while Black maneuvers pieces for a kingside attack.

results.name: Nxe4 Premature Grab
results.pgn: e4 e5 Nf3 Nc6 Bc4 Bc5 c3 Nf6 d4 exd4 cxd4 Nxe4
flags: WEAK_TRAP: student only -1 material, no mate (kind=trap, expected ≥ +3 or mate)
STUDENT_NOT_PUNISHER: trap PGN ends with opponent move (last SAN: Nxe4); student never plays the punishment in this line
results.explanation: If Black grabs e4 too early, d5 attacks the knight. The resulting tactical sequence wins material and gives White a powerful initiative.

results.name: Pawn Grab Recovery
results.pgn: d4 Nf6 c4 e6 g3 d5 Bg2 dxc4 Qa4+ Nbd7 Qxc4 a6 Nf3 b5 Qc2 Bb7 O-O c5 Bg5
results.explanation: Black grabs the c4 pawn but Qa4+ recovers it immediately while developing the queen actively. The Bg2 pressure on the long diagonal makes holding the pawn impossible.

results.name: b5 Chain Undermined
results.pgn: d4 Nf6 c4 e6 g3 d5 Bg2 Be7 Nf3 O-O O-O dxc4 Qc2 b5 a4 b4 Nbd2 Nd5
results.explanation: If Black tries to hold c4 with b5, a4 undermines the pawn chain. After Nxc4 the Bg2 diagonal opens and White recovers the pawn with a superior position.

results.name: Premature e5 Punished
results.pgn: e4 c5 Nf3 d6 d4 cxd4 Nxd4 Nf6 Nc3 a6 Bc4 e5 Nf5
results.explanation: If White pushes e5 prematurely or plays Nf5 after e5, Black gets active piece play challenging the central pawns. The pawn structure favors Black.

results.name: Nd5 Premature Jump
results.pgn: e4 c5 Nf3 d6 d4 cxd4 Nxd4 Nf6 Nc3 a6 Bc4 e6 Nd5
results.explanation: If White jumps Nd5 prematurely, Black exchanges knights and equalizes comfortably with natural development and active bishops.

results.name: Nxe4 Refutation
results.pgn: e4 e5 Nf3 Nc6 Bb5 a6 Ba4 Nf6 O-O Nxe4 d4 b5 Bb3 d5 dxe5 Be6 c3 Bc5 Nbd2 O-O Bc2 Nxd2 Qxd2
results.explanation: If Black grabs the e4 pawn early with Nxe4, White plays d4 opening the center while Black's knight is offside. Re1 can come with tempo pinning the knight and White gets strong initiative.

results.name: Bird's Defense Refutation
results.pgn: e4 e5 Nf3 Nc6 Bb5 Nd4 Nxd4 exd4 O-O
results.explanation: Bird's Defense (3...Nd4) is a known dubious sideline: trading the knight on d4 leaves Black with a doubled isolated d-pawn and a tempo deficit. After 4.Nxd4 exd4 5.O-O, White finishes development unmolested while Black still has to solve the cramped d-pawn. ECO C61 (Ruy Lopez: Bird Variation).

results.name: c4 Pawn Grab Recovery
results.pgn: d4 Nf6 c4 e6 g3 d5 Bg2 dxc4 Nf3 a6 O-O Nc6 e3 Bd7 Qe2 b5 a4 Rb8
results.explanation: Black grabs the c4 pawn and tries to hold with b5 and a6, but White undermines the structure with a4. The Bg2 applies crushing pressure down the long diagonal.

results.name: Diagonal Pin Trap
results.pgn: d4 Nf6 c4 e6 g3 d5 Bg2 Be7 Nf3 O-O O-O dxc4 Ne5 Nc6 Bxc6 bxc6 Nxc6 Qe8 Nxe7+ Qxe7 Qa4
flags: WEAK_TRAP: student only +0 material, no mate (kind=trap, expected ≥ +3 or mate)
results.explanation: After dxc4, White plays Ne5 and if Black develops Nc6, the Bg2 creates a devastating pin. White wins material by exploiting the long diagonal pressure.

results.name: White Overextends with f4
results.pgn: e4 c5 Nf3 d6 d4 cxd4 Nxd4 Nf6 Nc3 a6 f4 e5 Nf3 Nbd7
results.explanation: If White overextends with f4, Black plays e5 + Nbd7 — the weakened e4 pawn becomes a target as Black builds queenside counterplay.

results.name: e5 Fork Timing
results.pgn: e4 c5 Nf3 d6 d4 cxd4 Nxd4 Nf6 Nc3 a6 Be2 e5 Nb3 Be7 O-O O-O Be3 Be6 f4 exf4 Bxf4 Nc6 Kh1 d5
results.explanation: Black times the e5 push to gain central space, then follows up with d5 when White's pieces are committed. The central break equalizes and gives Black active piece play.

results.name: Marshall d5 Prevented
results.pgn: e4 e5 Nf3 Nc6 Bb5 a6 Ba4 Nf6 O-O Be7 Re1 b5 Bb3 O-O c3 d5 exd5 Nxd5 Nxe5 Nxe5 Rxe5 c6 d4 Bd6 Re1 Qh4 g3 Qh3 Be3 Bg4 Qd3 Rae8 Nd2
results.explanation: White takes on d5 to defuse the Marshall Attack. After Nxe5 Rxe5, White has a strong central position. The d4 pawn gives White space and Black must play precisely to avoid a disadvantage.

results.name: Premature d5 Punished
results.pgn: e4 e5 Nf3 Nc6 Bb5 a6 Ba4 Nf6 O-O Be7 Re1 b5 Bb3 O-O c3 d5 exd5 e4
results.explanation: If Black pushes d5 but follows with e4 prematurely, White plays Bc2 + Qf3 with open lines and a strong attack on the weakened light squares.

results.name: White's Premature Kingside Attack
results.pgn: e4 c5 Nf3 d6 d4 cxd4 Nxd4 Nf6 Nc3 a6 Be3 e5 Nb3 Be6 f3 Be7 Qd2 O-O O-O-O Nbd7 g4 b5 g5 b4 gxf6 bxc3 Qxc3
results.explanation: If White pushes g5 too aggressively, Black sacrifices the knight and after bxc3 Qxc3, Black has powerful bishops and open lines for the impending attack.

results.name: e5 Central Trick
results.pgn: e4 c5 Nf3 d6 d4 cxd4 Nxd4 Nf6 Nc3 a6 Bc4 e5 Nf3 Be7 O-O
results.explanation: After Bc4 e5, the knight retreats and Black gets a comfortable position. The e5 push gains central space and prepares the typical Najdorf queenside expansion with b5.

results.name: Exchange Nxc3 Trick
results.pgn: d4 Nf6 c4 g6 Nc3 d5 cxd5 Nxd5 e4 Nxc3 bxc3 Bg7 Bc4 c5 Ne2 O-O O-O Nc6 Be3 Bg4 f3 Na5 Bd3 cxd4 cxd4 Be6
results.explanation: In the Exchange Grunfeld, Black plays Nxc3 giving White the center but targeting the weak doubled c-pawns. After c5 and Nc6-Na5, Black undermines White's center and the Bg7 becomes a monster.

results.name: Center Overextension
results.pgn: d4 Nf6 c4 g6 Nc3 d5 cxd5 Nxd5 e4 Nxc3 bxc3 Bg7 f4 c5
results.explanation: If White overextends with e4, f4, and d5, Black targets the overextended pawns with c5 — the Bg7 pressures the center from the flank as the structure cracks.

results.name: Oh No My Queen Trap
results.pgn: e4 e5 Nf3 Nf6 Nxe5 Nc6 Nxc6 dxc6 d3 Bc5
flags: WEAK_TRAP: student only -1 material, no mate (kind=trap, expected ≥ +3 or mate)
results.explanation: The signature Rosen trap setup. After d3 Bc5, if White plays Bg5 hoping to trade pieces, the Stafford's signature Bxd8 'Oh No My Queen' resource awaits — Bxf2+ followed by Bg4+ leads to either checkmate or winning White's queen back.

results.name: Englund Queen Pressure
results.pgn: d4 e5 dxe5 Nc6 Nf3 Qe7 Bf4 Qb4+ Bd2 Qxb2 Nc3 Bb4 Rb1 Qa3 Nd5 Bxd2+ Qxd2 Nxe5 Nxe5 Qe7
results.explanation: After dxe5 Nc6 and Qe7, Black puts immediate pressure on the e5 pawn. White must be careful or the queen infiltrates with Qb4+ grabbing the b2 pawn and creating chaos.

results.name: Englund Queen Trap
results.pgn: d4 e5 dxe5 Nc6 Nf3 Qe7 Qd5 f6 exf6 Nxf6 Qb3 d5 Bg5 Rb8 Nc3 Nb4 O-O-O Nc2 Bxf6 Qxf6 Nd5 Qd6 Qxc2
results.explanation: In the Englund queen trap variation, Black lures White's queen to d5 and plays f6 to open lines. If White is not careful, the knight lands on b4 threatening Nc2 forking the king and rook.

results.name: Nh5 Bishop Chase Refuted
results.pgn: d4 d5 Bf4 Nf6 e3 e6 Nf3 Nh5 Bg5
results.explanation: If Black chases the bishop with Nh5, White simply plays Bg5 switching targets. The knight on h5 is misplaced and White gets comfortable development while Black wastes time.

results.name: Premature c5 Refuted
results.pgn: d4 d5 Bf4 Nf6 e3 c5 c3 Nc6 Nd2 e6 Ngf3 Bd6 Bg3 O-O Bd3 Qe7 Ne5 Nd7 f4 f6 Nxc6 bxc6 O-O
results.explanation: If Black plays c5 too early, White holds the center with c3 and the d4 pawn stays firm. The London structure proves resilient and White gets a comfortable position with Ne5 outpost.

results.name: Scholar's Mate Counter-Attack
results.pgn: e4 e5 Nf3 Nc6 Bc4 Bc5 Ng5
results.explanation: If White goes for a Scholar's mate-style Qh5 or Ng5 attack, Black defends with Qe7 + Nf6 gaining tempo on the queen. Black turns the early queen sortie into a development advantage.

results.name: Nf6 Fork Trick
results.pgn: e4 e5 Nf3 Nc6 Bc4 Bc5 c3 Nf6 d4 exd4 cxd4 Bb4+ Nc3 Nxe4 O-O
results.explanation: A sacrificial attack on f7 — Bxf7+ followed by piece activity, not a forced material win. After Nxe4 + O-O, White's open lines and active pieces compensate for the material; the student should be told this is positional pressure, not 'trap Black's queen'.

results.name: Qh4 Exploitation
results.pgn: e4 e5 Nf3 Nc6 d4 exd4 Nxd4 Qh4 Nb5 Qxe4+ Be2
results.explanation: After Nxd4 Qh4, Black's queen grabs the e4 pawn but White plays Nb5 threatening Nc7. Black must spend time retreating while White develops with tempo and castles quickly.

results.name: Bc5 Fork Setup
results.pgn: e4 e5 Nf3 Nc6 d4 exd4 Nxd4 Bc5 Nxc6 Qf6 Qf3 Qxf3 gxf3 bxc6 Nc3 Nf6
results.explanation: After Bc5 Nxc6, if Black plays Qf6 White trades queens and gets a superior endgame. The doubled f-pawns and active rooks give White a lasting structural advantage.

results.name: Accepted with Strong Center
results.pgn: e4 e5 f4 exf4 Nf3 g5 Bc4 Bg7 O-O d6 d4 h6 c3 Nf6
flags: WEAK_TRAP: student only -1 material, no mate (kind=trap, expected ≥ +3 or mate)
STUDENT_NOT_PUNISHER: trap PGN ends with opponent move (last SAN: Nf6); student never plays the punishment in this line
results.explanation: After exf4, White develops rapidly with Nf3, Bc4, and O-O. The open f-file and central control set up the typical King's Gambit Bxf7+ attacking themes against Black's exposed king.

results.name: d5 Knight Catch
results.pgn: e4 e5 f4 d5 exd5 e4 Nc3 Nf6 d3 Bb4 Bd2 exd3 Bxd3 O-O
results.explanation: If Black declines with d5, White captures and opens the center. After Nc3 and d3, White undermines Black's advanced pawn for active piece play and open lines.

results.name: Premature h4 by White Punished
results.pgn: e4 c6 d4 d5 Nc3 dxe4 Nxe4 Bf5 Ng3 Bg6 h4 h5 Nf3 Nd7 Bd3 Bxd3 Qxd3
results.explanation: If White plays h4 too aggressively without proper preparation, Black responds with ...h5 freezing the h-pawn — then standard development gives Black a perfectly safe position with the kingside permanently weakened.

results.name: Legal's Mate Setup
results.pgn: e4 e5 Nf3 Nc6 Bc4 d6 Nc3 Bg4 h3
flags: WEAK_TRAP: student only +0 material, no mate (kind=trap, expected ≥ +3 or mate)
results.explanation: The setup for Legal's Mate. If Black insists on the Bg4 pin and White plays h3, Black's natural Bh5 (instead of trading) allows the famous Nxe5! queen sacrifice followed by Bxf7+ Ke7 Nd5#.

results.name: d4 Center Trick
results.pgn: e4 e5 Nf3 Nc6 Bc4 Bc5 d4 Bxd4 Nxd4 Nxd4 O-O
results.explanation: White sacrifices the d4 pawn to lure the bishop and after Nxd4, castles with tempo. The open center and f4 push create dangerous attacking chances against Black's exposed king.

results.name: Premature White Attack
results.pgn: e4 c5 Nf3 d6 d4 cxd4 Nxd4 Nf6 Nc3 a6 Be3 e5 Nb3 Be6 f3 Be7 Qd2 O-O O-O-O b5 Kb1 b4
flags: WEAK_TRAP: student only +0 material, no mate (kind=trap, expected ≥ +3 or mate)
results.explanation: If White castles queenside and plays passively, Black pushes b5-b4 driving the knight away. The Na4 + Nxe4 follow-up exploits the uncoordinated white pieces.

results.name: e5 Fork Trick
results.pgn: e4 c5 Nf3 d6 d4 cxd4 Nxd4 Nf6 Nc3 a6 Be2 e5 Nb3 Be7 O-O O-O Be3 Be6 Qd2 Nbd7 a4 Rc8 a5
results.explanation: Black times e5 to push the knight back, then plans d5 at the right moment to break open the center. The a5 setup confines White's queenside pieces ahead of the central break.

results.name: f3 Punished by d5
results.pgn: d4 Nf6 c4 e6 Nc3 Bb4 f3 d5 a3 Bxc3+ bxc3 c5 cxd5 exd5 e3 O-O Bd3 b6 Ne2 Ba6 Bxa6 Nxa6 O-O Nc7
results.explanation: If White plays the aggressive f3 Samisch, Black strikes with d5 in the center. The doubled c-pawns become a permanent weakness and Black gets excellent play against White's compromised structure.

results.name: c5 Timing Trick
results.pgn: d4 Nf6 c4 e6 Nc3 Bb4 e3 O-O Bd3 c5 Nf3 d5 O-O dxc4 Bxc4 cxd4 exd4 b6 Bg5 Bb7 Re1 Nbd7 Rc1 Rc8 Qb3 Be7
results.explanation: Black times c5 perfectly to challenge White's center. After dxc4 and cxd4, White is left with an isolated d-pawn that becomes a target. Black develops naturally and applies pressure.
